title: Sets & Tuples
beginnerExplanation: Sets store unique unordered elements (no duplicates). Tuples are like lists but immutable — once created, they can't be changed.
intermediateExplanation: Sets eliminate duplicates instantly and enable fast membership tests. Tuples protect data from accidental changes and can be used as dictionary keys.

realWorldAnalogy.title: Guest List vs. Time Capsule
realWorldAnalogy.description: A set is like a guest list where each name appears only once, regardless of how many times you add it. A tuple is a time capsule — once sealed, its contents are forever fixed.

mentalModel: A set is like a guest list where each name appears only once, regardless of how many times you add it. A tuple is a time capsule — once sealed, its contents are forever fixed.

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Trying to create an empty set with {} — that creates a dict! Use set() instead.
Attempting to modify a tuple (e.g., my_tuple[0] = 5) — tuples are immutable.
Trying to add mutable items (like lists) to a set — only hashable items allowed.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Sets & Tuples but hit a common pitfall: Trying to create an empty set with {} — that creates a dict! Use set() instead.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Use sets when uniqueness matters or for fast membership checks (x in my_set).

practiceQuestions.question: How do you create an empty set in Python?
practiceQuestions.explanation: {} creates an empty dictionary, not a set. Use set() for an empty set.

developerMode.industryUsage: Sets & Tuples is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Use sets when uniqueness matters or for fast membership checks (x in my_set).
Use tuples for fixed collections like coordinates, RGB colors, or database rows.
Remember: sets are unordered — don't rely on element positions.
antiPatterns: Trying to create an empty set with {} — that creates a dict! Use set() instead.
Attempting to modify a tuple (e.g., my_tuple[0] = 5) — tuples are immutable.
Trying to add mutable items (like lists) to a set — only hashable items allowed.
